title: Master Johnnyï؟½s Next-Door Neighbor
content:
آ آ آ آ It was spring the first time that I saw her, for her papa and mamma moved in
آ آ آ آ Next door, just as skating was over, and marbles about to begin;
آ آ آ آ For the fence in our back yard was broken, and I saw, as I peeped through the slat,
آ آ آ آ There were ï؟½Johnny-jump-upsï؟½ all around her, and I knew it was spring just by that.

آ آ آ آ I never knew whether she saw me, for she didnï؟½t say nothing to me,
آ آ آ آ But ï؟½Ma! hereï؟½s a slat in the fence broke, and the boy that is next door can see.ï؟½
آ آ آ آ But the next day I climbed on our wood-shed, as you know Mamma says Iï؟½ve a right,
آ آ آ آ And she calls out, ï؟½Well, peekinï؟½ is manners!ï؟½ and I answered her, ï؟½Sass is perlite!ï؟½

آ آ آ آ But I wasnï؟½t a bit mad, no, Papa, and to prove it, the very next day,
آ آ آ آ When she ran past our fence in the morning I happened to get in her way,
آ آ آ آ For you know I am ï؟½chunkedï؟½ and clumsy, as she says are all boys of my size,
آ آ آ آ And she nearly upset me, she did, Pa, and laughed till tears came in her eyes.

آ آ آ آ And then we were friends from that moment, for I knew that she told Kitty Sage,
آ آ آ آ And she wasnï؟½t a girl that would flatter ï؟½that she thought I was tall for my age.ï؟½
آ آ آ آ And I gave her four apples that evening, and took her to ride on my sled,
آ آ آ آ And ï؟½What am I telling you this for?ï؟½ Why, Papa, my neighbor is dead!

آ آ آ آ You donï؟½t hear one half I am saying, I really do think itï؟½s too bad!
آ آ آ آ Why, you might have seen crape on her door-knob, and noticed to-day Iï؟½ve been sad.
آ آ آ آ And theyï؟½ve got her a coffin of rosewood, and they say they have dressed her in white,
آ آ آ آ And Iï؟½ve never once looked through the fence, Pa, since she died at eleven last night.

آ آ آ آ And Ma says itï؟½s decent and proper, as I was her neighbor and friend,
آ آ آ آ That I should go there to the funeral, and she thinks that you ought to attend;
آ آ آ آ But I am so clumsy and awkward, I know I shall be in the way,
آ آ آ آ And suppose they should speak to me, Papa, I wouldnï؟½t know just what to say.

آ آ آ آ So I think I will get up quite early, I know I sleep late, but I know
آ آ آ آ Iï؟½ll be sure to wake up if our Bridget pulls the string that Iï؟½ll tie to my toe;
آ آ آ آ And Iï؟½ll crawl through the fence, and Iï؟½ll gather the ï؟½Johnny-jump-upsï؟½ as they grew
آ آ آ آ Round her feet the first day that I saw her, and, Papa, Iï؟½ll give them to you.

آ آ آ آ For youï؟½re a big man, and, you know, Pa, can come and go just where you choose,
آ آ آ آ And youï؟½ll take the flowers in to her, and surely theyï؟½ll never refuse;
آ آ آ آ But, Papa, donï؟½t say theyï؟½re from Johnny; they wonï؟½t understand, donï؟½t you see?
آ آ آ آ But just lay them down on her bosom, and, Papa, sheï؟½ll know theyï؟½re from Me.
meaning:
This evocative piece by Bret Harte (Francis) represents a masterful exploration of classic. The lines capture a profound emotional resonance, inviting the reader to reflect on the deeper themes of human experience and artistic expression.

In the broader context of Bret Harte (Francis)'s bibliography, these specific lines stand out for their clarity and poignancy. Whether you are searching for inspiration or a moment of quiet contemplation, this work offers a timeless perspective that transcends its original era.

At Linespedia, we have curated this specific poem to ensure it reaches a modern audience. We recommend sharing these lines as a digital poster or using them as a thoughtful caption to connect with others who appreciate the enduring beauty of verse.
